blocks.headline: Does Your Case Need Expert Evidence?
blocks.subtitle: Many cases benefit from expert evidence. Others don't. In 2 minutes, we'll tell you whether expert witness assessment would strengthen your case — and what type.

blocks.heading: When Expert Evidence Makes a Difference

rows.col2: Court needs professional assessment of ability to parent, mental health, coping
Expert can assess attachment, child's needs, safeguarding concerns
Psychologist assesses trauma, PTSD, functional impact, prognosis
Psychiatrist/psychologist assesses defendant's mental state and capacity
Depends on whether psychological injury or capability is central
Psychiatrist assesses trauma, torture sequelae, medical and psychological impact
Expert evidence rarely adds value unless mental capacity is questioned
Expert evidence not relevant unless mental state is central

tabs.label: When You Absolutely Need an Expert

children.text: Expert witness evidence is essential in cases where:
Capacity is in question — Is a parent able to look after their child? Is someone fit to plead?
Mental health is central — Domestic abuse, PTSD, depression, psychosis require expert diagnosis
Behaviour must be assessed — Is someone aggressive? Do they pose a risk?
The court/tribunal lacks expertise — Judges need expert help with complex psychological issues
The dispute hinges on psychology — Criminal, family, and asylum cases often need expert evidence
Examples:

Care proceedings where the court must decide whether a parent can care for their child safely. Criminal trials where a defendant claims unfitness to plead due to intellectual disability. Asylum claims where the applicant suffered torture and expert assessment of trauma is central to the claim.

tabs.label: When Expert Evidence Can Strengthen Your Case

children.text: Expert witness evidence is valuable but not essential when:
It adds credibility — Expert opinion reinforces your narrative with professional evidence
It clarifies complexity — An expert can untangle psychological factors in disputed cases
It preempts the other side — The other party may instruct an expert; getting yours in first protects your position
It satisfies procedural rules — Some courts expect expert evidence on certain issues
Examples:

A personal injury claim where expert psychological assessment of trauma strengthens the damages claim. An employment tribunal where expert evidence of stress-related illness adds weight. A family law case where an expert report on a child's attachment and wishes adds persuasive weight.

tabs.label: When You Probably Don't Need an Expert

children.text: Expert evidence is unlikely to help when:
The dispute is purely legal or financial — Contract interpretation, property boundary, debt
The facts are undisputed — Everyone agrees what happened; the disagreement is about legal consequence
Psychology is tangential — The case is primarily about something else
However: if the other side may instruct an expert, consider it anyway for balance.

blocks.heading: How Expert Witnesses Changed Case Outcomes

caseStudies.title: Parenting Capacity Was Central
caseStudies.description: A family law firm was unsure whether parenting capacity assessment was necessary. The judge required it. Expert psychology assessment showed the parent could provide safe care with support — outcomes that influenced the court's decision.

caseStudies.title: Fitness to Plead — Without Which, No Trial
caseStudies.description: A criminal defendant claimed unfitness to plead. Expert psychiatric assessment confirmed genuine fitness concerns. The case didn't proceed to trial; instead, the defendant received appropriate mental health support.

caseStudies.title: Psychological Injury Claim — Quantified Damages
caseStudies.description: A PI claim for psychological injury seemed weak until a psychologist's report documented PTSD, functional impairment, and long-term prognosis. This strengthened the damages claim substantially.

faqs.question: How do I know if I really need an expert?
faqs.answer: Use our Suitability Checker above (takes 2 minutes). Or call us on 01306 879 975. Our Client Managers have 15+ years' experience and can assess whether your case warrants expert evidence based on a brief discussion — at no cost.

faqs.question: What if I instruct an expert and the court doesn't use their evidence?
faqs.answer: It happens, but it's rare if the expert is well-matched and well-instructed. Our vetting and matching process is rigorous and we ensure the Letter of Instruction is clear. If you're concerned, discuss the case specifics with our team before instructing.

faqs.question: Is expert evidence more important in family law or criminal law?
faqs.answer: Both matter. In family law, capacity and parenting assessments are often essential. In criminal law, fitness to plead and dangerousness assessments are mandatory in many cases. In civil/PI, expert evidence strengthens damages claims. In employment, it depends on the claim.

faqs.question: Can I instruct an expert 'just in case' if I'm unsure?
faqs.answer: You can, but it's not necessary. Instead, discuss your case with us first. If we think an expert would help, we'll recommend it. If not, we'll be honest about that. We don't over-service.

faqs.question: What if the other side instructs an expert — do I need one too?
faqs.answer: Very likely, yes. If your opponent has expert evidence supporting their position, having your own expert balance it is usually wise.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to get expert guidance on whether I need an expert?
faqs.answer: It's free. Use the Suitability Checker above. Or call 01306 879 975 and speak to a Client Manager. No obligation, no charge.

faqs.question: What if my case is unusual or niche?
faqs.answer: Even more reason to discuss with us. We have 1,000+ experts across all disciplines and sub-specialisms. If expert evidence would help your niche case, we can find the right specialist. If it won't help, we'll tell you that too.

faqs.question: Do courts expect expert evidence in certain case types?
faqs.answer: Yes. Family law cases involving children usually benefit from expert assessment. Criminal cases involving fitness to plead require it. Asylum cases involving torture survivors often require psychiatric assessment. In other areas, it's discretionary but often strengthens claims.

blocks.text: Not all cases need expert evidence. We'll give you honest guidance on whether it would help yours — and if it won't, we'll say so.
